assignment_description:
World Geography
Russia
⍞ Materials: Prisoners of Geography

➜ INTRO
To aid communication throughout this course, we will call the big blue Prisoners of Geography: Our World Explained in 12 Simple Maps book simply your "map book," and we will call Prisoners of Geography: Ten Maps That Explain Everything About the World your "Prisoners of Geography" book.

➜ MAP WORK
Print one blank map of Russia.
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of Russia

Using the blank map as a pattern to trace or as a guide, sketch a rough map of Russia in your notebook. As in the sample sketch maps at the link, the basic shape is the objective rather than detailed perfection. If an alternative to sketching or tracing is needed, students may use their finger to trace the blank map. In this way, a blank map may be substituted for the sketch as needed throughout these lessons. Like imagining shapes in clouds, consider what the shape of the map looks like to help you remember your mental sketch.
∞ Image Link: Sketch maps

Using your atlas, globe, phone, and/or p.8-9 in your Prisoners of Geography book, locate the following items. Add to your map and label as many as you can reasonably recall:

•Moscow •St. Petersburg •Murmansk •Arctic Circle •Ural Mountains •Lake Baikal •Siberia •Volga River •Crimea •Vladivostok •Anything else you would like

➜ READ & NARRATE
Read aloud or silently.
Prisoners of Geography p.1-9 (Introduction)

★ STUDENT TIP
Make a point to know and look up something about places mentioned in the current news and events of the day. Look up place names in your atlas. Perhaps make a map to identify event locations throughout the year.

Historical Geography
⍞ Materials: All Over the Map, geography notebook

➜ READ
All Over the Map, 9 pages a week

➜ WRITE
Compose 5 questions and/or 5 reflections about the map. Also, write down any ideas that come to mind about maps you could draw based on the ones you are studying. For example, you may be inspired to draw a map showing the relative lengths of all the rivers and streams near you after looking at the map on p.23.

Be sure to turn some of your ideas into reality over the term.

World Geography
Russia Continued
⍞ Materials: Prisoners of Geography, map book

➜ MAP WORK
Read p.8-11 in your map book. Using your atlas, globe, phone, and/or your book, locate the following and add from them to your map:

•Trans-Siberian Railway •Bering Sea •Arctic Ocean •Pacific Ocean •Caspian Sea •Black Sea •Baltic Sea •Caucasus Mountains •North European Plain (p.9 in your map book) •Sevastopol •Anything else you would like
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of the World

➜ READ & NARRATE
Read aloud or silently, referencing your map as needed
Prisoners of Geography p.11-24 (first half of Russia)

★ STUDENT TIP
Remember to label what you want to recall.

➜ READ

➜ WRITE

Be sure to turn some of your ideas into reality over the term.

World Geography
Russia Continued
⍞ Materials: Prisoners of Geography, map book

➜ MEMORY SKETCH
Sketch a rough map of Russia and see what you can fill in without looking at your map. If you have been using blank maps, you may continue to do so, or you can try a rough sketch now!

➜ MAP WORK
Read p.12-15 in your map book. Locate/add to your map:

•Bering Strait •Add the border countries •Anything else you would like

➜ READ & NARRATE
Read aloud or silently, referencing your map
Prisoners of Geography p.25-37 (second half of Russia)

• DISCUSS
If you are working independently, plan a time this week to go to your teacher, show them your map, and share something you are learning about this region of the world. If interested, you could create and share a travelog with illustrations or images that represent important ideas about the country or a travel poster created on Canva or another computer program. If working in a class, share with your classmates and teacher.

➜ MAP WORK
Print one blank map of USA
∞ Map Link: Blank Map of USA

See how well you can sketch a map of the US without using the blank map. Then refine your sketch, if needed, using the map. Draw and label as many states as you can from memory as well as rivers, mountains, and cities. Remember Alaska and Hawaii! Don't worry if you don't get the sizes or outlines right. It is a rough sketch.

➜ READ
p.24-25 in your map book. Use the map to fill in what you would like. We are going to skip p.26-29. Read on your own if interested.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Read aloud or silently, referencing your map
Prisoners of Geography p.68-79 (first half of US)

∞ Video Link: 1800s U.S. History & John C. Fremont

If time permits, watch two short videos that provide additional insight into how America expanded to the west coast.

∞ Video Link: 1800s U.S. History
∞ Video Link: John C. Fremont

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geography: Grade 9 — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Prisoners of Geography: Sketch a map with as many details as you can of Russia, China, the US, Canada, or Europe. Explain how the geography of the area shown on your map helps us understand some of the historical, political, economic, or other issues of the area.

· All Over the Map: Write about one of your favorite maps in All Over the Map, and write about a map that you created.

➜ READ

•Sahara Desert •Indian Ocean •Red Sea •Mediterranean Sea •Atlantic Ocean •Cape of Good Hope •Suez Canal •Sahel •Gambia •Niger •Chad •Eritrea •Congo •Central African Republic •Uganda • Tanzania •Angola •Namibia •Zambezi RIver •Victoria Falls •Anything else you would like

➜ READ & NARRATE
Read aloud or silently, referencing your map. Stop after reading p.117 and look at the link on map distortion before finishing the reading.
Prisoners of Geography p.115-123 (first part of Africa)
∞ Article Link: Maps Distort How We See the World

★ STUDENT TIP
You may continue mapping using the same method or try a new one! The purpose is to help you remember and think about the geography of the country.

assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geography: Grade 9 — TERM 3 EXAM QUESTIONS
· Prisoners of Geography: Sketch a map with as many details as you can of Korea and Japan, Latin America, or the Arctic. Explain how the geography of the area shown on your map helps us understand some of the historical, political, economic, or other issues of the area.

· What Linnaeus Saw: Summarize the contributions of Linnaeus to science, AND give a detailed account of a favorite part of the book.
